title: The Weight of One Shirt
author: Emerson Sam Navaya
content:
The journey from Matiya to Zomba began before sunrise.
I boarded the rickety minibus at our trading centre, its metal frame rattling like an old man clearing his throat. We passed through Jali, rolled slowly past Pirimiti Health Centre, and joined the winding road that seemed to stretch endlessly toward a future I could neither fully imagine nor afford. In my lap rested a thin, fading plastic bag containing everything I owned for university: one pair of trousers, one shirt, and a pair of worn-out shoes whose cracked soles had already travelled farther than most dreams from my village ever dared.
As the bus groaned toward Zomba Bus Stage, I stared through the dusty window and wondered if hope was supposed to feel this heavy.
I came from Matiya, a place where life often teaches you to adjust your dreams to fit your circumstances. But mine had refused to shrink.
When I finally arrived at Chancellor College, the university looked enormous- its tall buildings draped in history, prestige, and quiet intimidation. Students moved across campus with the kind of confidence that comes from familiarity and comfort. Their clothes were crisp, their laughter easy, their steps certain.
I stood among them feeling like a misplaced paragraph in an otherwise perfect book.
That first night in the hostel was not filled with excitement, as many imagine university life to be.
It was filled with arithmetic.
I counted the money in my pocket once, then again, then a third time, as though persistence might somehow multiply it. It never did. Lying on the thin mattress, staring at the ceiling above me, I realised that getting into university had been the easier battle.
Staying there would be the real test.
Classes began, and I quickly discovered that hunger sharpens the mind even as it weakens the body.
I attended every lecture with near-religious devotion, partly because I was determined to succeed, and partly because lecture halls offered something I could not buy elsewhere- a temporary escape from my reality. Inside those rooms, knowledge belonged to everyone equally.
For a few precious hours, nobody cared that my shirt was the same one I had worn the day before.
Or the day before that.
Every evening, I washed it quietly in the hostel bathroom. It became a ritual, almost sacred. I would scrub carefully, wring it out, and hang it by the window, praying it would dry before morning.
Most mornings, it didn’t.
I wore it anyway.
The cold dampness against my skin always carried me back home- to the river in Matiya where we washed clothes, to my mother’s weary hands, to my father’s heavy silence whenever money ran out.
My parents did everything they could.
But effort and provision are not always the same thing.
We carried the burden of my education together like people balancing a fragile plate- each of us holding a corner, hoping it would not shatter.
Sometimes it nearly did.
There were days when meals became a luxury I could not afford. On such days, I drank water slowly, convincing my stomach to mistake fullness for nourishment. I learned to avoid places where students gathered to eat, not because I lacked hunger, but because I lacked the courage to watch others satisfy theirs.
My first year passed like a storm- loud, relentless, unforgiving.
I survived it.
But survival is not the same as living.
Then came the second year.
Something shifted.
Perhaps it was familiarity. Perhaps it was the quiet rebellion of a spirit that had grown tired of shrinking itself.
Whatever it was, it changed me.
I began speaking more in class. At first, my voice trembled as though uncertain it deserved to be heard. But words, once given space, have a way of discovering their own confidence.
I found refuge in literature, philosophy, and history.
Books became both shelter and weapon.
I read not merely to pass examinations, but to understand the world and my place within it.
Before long, lecturers began to notice.
“Your analysis is unusually sharp,” one remarked after class.
Another returned my essay with words I still remember: You have a rare voice. Protect it.
Those simple acknowledgments changed something inside me.
Soon, fellow students began seeking me out- for help with assignments, for debates that stretched late into the night, for conversations about ideas larger than ourselves.
For the first time, I was no longer just surviving.
I was becoming.
Within the Arts Department, my name began to carry quiet weight.
And then there was her.
She was a Fine Arts lecturer- graceful, composed, with a presence that seemed to soften every room she entered. Her authority was never loud, yet it was unmistakable.
She noticed things others missed.
Not just in paintings.
In people.
Our first meaningful conversation happened after one of her lectures.
“You think deeply,” she said, studying me with a kind of attentiveness I was unaccustomed to.
The words unsettled me.
Compliments had always felt like borrowed clothes- ill-fitting and undeserved.
“Thank you,” I muttered.
That was how it began.
Slowly.
Naturally.
What grew between us was not something immediate or easily defined. It was a friendship that led the way art reveals itself- layer by layer, each stroke deepening the picture.
She introduced me to worlds I had never imagined entering: quiet cafés, art exhibitions, thoughtful spaces where conversation flowed as freely as the coffee.
At first, I felt like an intruder.
She sensed it.
“You belong wherever your mind can reach,” she once told me.
For reasons I could not explain, I believed her.
Our conversations soon moved beyond academics.
We spoke of purpose, fear, ambition, and the invisible burdens people carry.
She listened in a way that made silence feel safe.
Then came the afternoon that changed everything.
She called unexpectedly.
“I’m not feeling well,” she said softly. “Could you come over and help me prepare something to eat?”
I hesitated.
Not because I didn’t want to help, but because cooking was hardly among my strengths.
“I can come,” I replied carefully, “but I cannot promise anything edible.”
She laughed.
“Just come.”
Her house was modest yet elegant, filled with paintings that seemed alive with quiet stories.
Standing there, I felt the familiar distance between her world and mine.
“I warned you,” I said.
“Then we will figure it out together,” she smiled.
That afternoon, somewhere between burnt onions and awkward laughter, I learned she was unmarried.
It was not announced.
It simply revealed itself in the natural pauses of conversation, in the solitude of the house, in the absence of another life woven into hers.
We cooked- or rather, improvised.
The meal was simple and imperfect.
Yet it remains one of the most memorable meals of my life.
Because we shared it not as lecturer and student, but as two people meeting beyond titles.
Our friendship deepened after that.
But with depth came complexity.
There were boundaries neither of us named, yet both understood.
Still, she remained steady.
One day she looked at me and said, “You are going to go far. Just never forget where you started.”
“I could never,” I replied.
And I meant every word.
Years later, I graduated at the top of my class.
Not because life had become easier, but because hardship had taught me discipline.
Opportunities followed-scholarships, research, recognition.
I left Zomba.
But Zomba never left me.
The damp shirt.
The empty stomach.
The silent tears of uncertainty.
They became the foundation on which I built everything.
And then, one day, I returned.
Not as the boy who arrived at Zomba Bus Stage clutching a plastic bag.
But as a lecturer.
Standing before students, some carrying struggles invisible to everyone but themselves, I saw reflections of who I had once been.
I told them what I had learned:
“Education is not determined by where you begin, but by how stubbornly you keep moving despite where you begin.”
Sometimes, walking across campus, I pass the hostel blocks and lecture halls that shaped me.
And sometimes, I think of her.
The Fine Arts lecturer who saw possibility in me long before I could recognise it myself.
Our paths eventually diverged, as all paths do.
But her influence remains.
Permanent.
Like a signature carefully etched into a masterpiece.
I never asked what we might have become.
Some relationships are not meant to be possessed.
They exist to awaken, transform, and guide.
She did all three.
Back in Matiya, my parents have grown older.
When I visit, we sit together beneath the same sky that once held all my fears.
“You’ve done well,” my father said to me one evening.
I smiled.
Not because I had arrived.
But because I had endured.
Because I had carried the weight of one shirt, one dream, and one uncertain beginning-
and somehow turned it into a life that once seemed impossible.
And in every lecture I deliver, in every student I encourage, I carry that boy with me.
The boy who left Matiya before sunrise, passed through Jali and Pirimiti, and arrived at Zomba Bus Stage with almost nothing.
The boy who had nothing- but refused to become nothing.

title: University Campus Life
author: Blessings Janah
content:
The villagers were very proud of Tiwonge, her peers admired her, some were jealous of her, Tiwonge was the first person to be selected to go to university, parents were so proud of her, after three-months Tiwonge started preparing to go to school, Tiwonge's parents advised her to work hard in school and not adopt any bad behaviors from people and that she should remember where she comes from and how difficult it was for her parents to make sure she should go to school, it was really very hard for her parents to afford for her to go to school because they were from a poor background but with Gods grace and the community's love, they were able to send her to go to the university. Tiwonge had applied for the Kwela fund , as excited as she was, she promised her parents that she will not disappoint them, she would continue to be a good and hardworking girl she has always been. After three days, Tiwonge had set off to start her first year at Malawi university of science and technology to study bachelor of science in chemical engineering. After reaching at Ndata campus she was allocated to sleep in campus and her mesho, Dorothy, studying Bachelors of Arts in Indigenous Knowledge was a fourth year student who semt to be friendly, she welcomed Tiwonge and told her good things about Malawi university of science and technology, for the past two months things were going good for tiwonge at school she used to talk to her parents over the phone promising to not disappoint them in anyway but she was struggling financially and her roommate noticed that she is struggling and she helped her by giving her 20,000 Tiwonge was thankful and she promised to repay when she is stable financially but Dorothy told her it is fine no need and told her it’s a gift for being a good roommate ,Tiwonge was amused and hugged her since then they become so close and called each other best friend , this other day Dorothy and Tiwonge went for a walk and they were having a conversation about their family background and Tiwonge found out that Dorothy was also from a poor family and this shocked her because Dorathy seem to be rich and she never lacked anything, Dorothy what do you mean you are poor like me tiwonge asked , Dorothy replied yes tiwonge I am ,tiwonge asked her so how come you have everything you need ,Dorothy laughed and said to her I promise you , you don’t need to know how I afford the life I am living now , but tiwonge insisted that she wants to know because she also want to live like her , innocently she asked her are you doing business or what please tell me I thought we are friends I also want be like please help me Dorothy she inserted ,Dorothy warned Tiwonge if I tell you the secret behind this and you tell anyone you will be in trouble and we won’t be friends anymore ,tiwonge replied fast and said no Dorothy I can never disappoint you and Dorothy told her that she will just school her what she does at night , this made her to be scared ,what do you mean at night is it witch craft? If it is please, it is fine tiwonge said, Dorothy laughed no its not witch craft I know we learn concept of witch craft but we are not witches please Dorothy said jokingly ,tiwonge laughed and after it clocked 7 pm Dorothy took tiwonge outside campus to show her what she does for money has promised and they stood long the road , tiwonge asked what are we waiting for here I thought you said you show me how you have money , patient tiwo you will see Dorothy said to her after 10 minutes a black expensive car stopped for the and the old man greeted them and asked Dorothy who is this friend of yours ,Dorothy replied this tiwonge my roommate , the old man asked is she going with us today she replied to him yes , tiwonge was confused who the man was but she acted natural and she and Dorothy entered the car and went out ,tiwonge went along but she never bothered of asking , and they went to a hotel , ate and the man took them back to school , after reaching to they hostel ,tiwonge asked who was is he your uncle and why didn’t you show me how you have money oh ! I get your uncle gives you the money right he seem to be very nice, Dorothy laughed oh innocent tiwo that’s my sugar daddy , tiwonge was shocked, yes tiwo that’s my man who provide everting for me ,tiwonge was in shock and she didn’t say a thing ,she just looked shocked ,Dorothy snapped please do judge me and please don’t tell anyone about this ,tiwonge promised her that she will not tell a single so about it.
After a month tiwonge stared admiring her roommate life style though her parents tried they best to give her what she needed but was not enough for her because she wanted to enjoy life like other girls in university , so he decided to ask Dorothy for help , Dorothy I am tired of this poverty life can you please help so I should be like you please , Dorothy was shocked what do you mean by that you want to be like me you are telling me you what to start dating old men for money are you sure about that ,I don’t want to be blamed in future if anything happens , tiwonge begged her no please help find me someone as well , I am done pretending of not wanting a good life like your life , I want to enjoy my university life , Dorothy accepted to help her after tiwonge begging ,okay okay don’t worry I will help you but don’t blame me if anything happens in future .
Tiwonge was introduced to a old man in his late 50s to be her sugar daddy but since she needed a soft life as her friend so bad she stared dating the man , the man use to provide everything she needed , she would go out with this man to expensive hotels , and she changed a lot she wasn’t the same humble ,kind ,respectful and hard working girl anymore she stared partying ,drinking and her grades went dawn ,after closing schools she lied to her parents that she will just stay at school because the holiday was too short and she went to spend the holiday at a hotel were the man she was dating MR Banda was paying for stay , MR Banda didn’t invite tiwonge to his house because he was married , after some weeks passed school results come out and she had failed two exams so she went back I n campus to write her supplementary exams and she passed them and she went back to the hotel too spend her holiday.
After one month passed it was reported on Malawi news that a girl has been killed by a woman because she found the girl in a hotel room with her husband , the whole village were shocked ,disappointed and sad after finding out it was their girl Tiwonge kandewere , questions were asked how that girl was so behaved , how come this , her parents were in big shock and they were disappointed and sad at the same time they found it hard to believe their well-behaved daughter would do this but yet they moaned their daughter Tiwonge.

title: My Walk Into A New Life
author: Wisdom VJ Msokwa
content:
The message lit up my phone as I stood beside my mother in the vegetable garden of my father’s house in Kasungu. The November sun was harsh.
Unknown Number texted me. It was around 12:43 PM.
“ The time for settlement has come. Prepare her .”
“Mother,” I said, my voice tight while rushing towards her, “Who is this? Someone has texted me but having a strange message.”
She stopped watering the chard, her back to me. Her shoulders, usually so straight, slumped. “It does not matter. It is a wrong number.”
“It mentions her. It knows you. Is it Father’s trouble?”
She finally turned. Her eyes were pools of a fear I had never seen before. “We are not thralls tied to a chain like slaves. We are caught,” she whispered, “in a trap with golden wires. It is better to walk a nailed bridge than to live in boiling water.”
Before I could ask what she meant, she walked quickly back to the house, leaving me with the buzzing phone and the silent, watching hills.
Three days later, the air in the nick of time, inside our reed-fenced house was thick enough to choke on. Father returned from the farm, the smell of dry earth and sweat clinging to him. He did not look at me.
“Daughter, Sankho,” he began, wiping his brow, his voice unnaturally soft. “As I stand before you now, clear in my own flesh, you must listen to a proposition.”
“What proposition?” The words shot out of me. “You called me here from school for a holiday, but no one speaks. Strange things are happening here but I don’t understand any. Just whispers and strange texts. Tell me.”
He cleared his throat, a long, rattling sound. He was gathering words when a firm, foreign knock echoed at the gate not the friendly tap of a neighbor.
My mother froze like a deadly living bird in its nest. Father’s eyes darted to the door.
“Go,” my mother hissed at me. “To your room. Now.”
“Why?” I asked with damn curiosity.
“Go!” She pushed me inside my room.
I retreated but lingered just inside the hallway. A man entered, dressed in a tailored golden jacket that looked absurd against our humble sitting room. His voice was smooth, like oil over stone. “The extension is over. Before two weeks are up, everything must be settled. You understand the terms. Make sure it doesn’t spray across the community. I will rather end your lives.”
I did not hear my father’s reply. Only a low murmur. I failed to breathe, my head over the sea.
Later, my mother came to my room. Her hands trembled as she clutched mine. “I have not choice. I must leave. Tonight.” I said to my mother.
“Stay here. You are causing troubles.” My mother convinced me.
“I have spoken to Madam Suwande, a lecturer at Ekwendeni. She will give ma a room till the opening of the semester. I think I’m not safe here.”
“Your father… he has debts. To that man. Mr. Zamunda.” Mother said to me as I was gathering my clothes to take my leave.
The pieces, cold and sharp, clicked into place. “What kind of debt is paid with a daughter?”
Her silence was the answer. I packed my single bag in the dark, my MSCE results, the 26 points that had won me my diploma in human rights at Livingstonia university, Ekwendeni campus folded at the bottom. We slipped out like shadows.
Ekwendeni Campus was a sanctuary of bustling students and open books. For one week, I breathed. Then, my father’s call came, a torrent of fury through the speaker. “You defy me? Your life is mine to direct! You have committed a great misconduct!” I held the phone away from my ear until the line went dead. My relief curdled into dread for my mother.
On the sixth evening, as my friends and I laughed outside the library, enjoying the campus life, a silver Toyota Sienta van pulled up. The door slid open. A red, pointed shoe touched the gravel. It was him, the golden jacket from my father’s house. Mr. Zamunda.
“Your parents are expecting you, get inside before you die laughing,” he said, not asking. His smile didn’t reach his eyes.
I responded though I was forced to start off with him, “why? What have I done wrong?”
The drive to his house in Kasungu city was silent. Inside, my parents sat on a stiff leather sofa. My mother would not look up. My father stood.
“Why am I here?” My voice was smaller than I wanted. I cried, my eyes out.
My father crossed the room in two strides. The slap cracked across my cheek, a white-hot burst of pain. “You left! You belong here now. You are to be a wife to Mr. Zamunda. He has been patient.”
“A wife?” The room tilted. “I have a diploma to finish. I don’t know him! We have never had an affair before.” I asked, with storms diving in the house.
Mr. Zamunda rose, smoothing his jacket. “Your parents borrowed forty million from me. A year ago. Business capital that vanished. The collateral was always you. It is signed and agreed.”
“No,” I whispered.
“It is our custom,” my father said, his voice hollow. “A failure of money is settled with a child. We even signed the agreement. You have to respect the power of culture.”
“Your school has been informed, you withdrew for marriage,” Mr. Zamunda said. “That story is already everywhere. Your new life is here.”
My father shocked me with his one last speech, “once you inform someone about this, I promise you over my dead body, I will end my life for good.”
A month into my husband’s house, which was a cage of silence and simmering anger, I woke to a sharp pain and the feel of blood. I shook him. “I’m bleeding.”
He shoved my hand away. “And? That is your body’s complaint.”
“It could be from… from last night. Something is wrong.”
He sat up, his face contorted. “You are always complaining! Be quiet!” His hand struck the side of my head.
The bleeding worsened. At the hospital, under the sterile lights, the doctor was gentle but firm. After the examination, she said the words: “Herpes Simplex. It is a sexually transmitted infection. Quite advanced.”
The world shrank to the sound of her voice. In the taxi home, I was a shell. When we entered his cold, tiled living room, I finally spoke. “The doctor said I have an infection. She said my partner must be told. That he likely has it.”
Mr. Zamunda poured himself a drink, the ice clinking. He did not look at me. “So? I have known for years. These things happen.”
He knew. For years. My father had traded me for a debt, and my husband had given me a lifelong sentence as a receipt. “my second wife died soon after she heard her contraction. She thought of telling her family and friends that made my anger overtook my smile, I later sent her to the new world where there is quite, just birds crying inside trees.” He warned me.
I walked to the small balcony, looking out at a city I did not know. The air of Kasungu rural, of Ekwendeni, of my childhood, was gone. I breathed in the heavy, polluted night. This was not a walk into a new life. It was an erasure of the old one. And somewhere, deep beneath the terror and the shame, a new, harder seed began to form. The walk was not over. It had just begun on a path I never chose, and my next step would have to be my own way out.

title: Peace Within Justice
author: Daniel Kabango
content:
I pack my things from my former office and take one last long look, the picture me and James took when he had just hired me a couple of months back. I leave it right there in the middle of the desk and carry my belongings box out of the building to Faith’s car and I turn back to say my final goodbye to James, he still did a lot for me and I respect that, but further than that there should not be any more favors. I knock on his door and am beckoned in and he takes a long look at me and sits down in his chair.
“There are a couple of firms that I know that would love a new addition to their teams and I could write a recommendation for wherever you go, if you would like that however, “he said.
“Don’t bother, I’ll find a way elsewhere.”
He sighed and looked at his computer just because he couldn’t look anywhere else.” I understand.”
I stare at him for a bit, I don’t see the honest, truth seeking man I looked up to when I was a student. I thought we could bring a different sense into the courtroom together, one where the future could be safe and everyone having a brighter future they deserve. I set my resignation on his desk and
I set my resignation on his desk and say to my friend, “Personally I really do thank you for the opportunity and faith you put in me but I apologize I couldn’t be of any further assistance to you. I resign.”
~~
“So I would like to introduce you formally to our newly founded law firm, J.G & Co. You’re definitely most welcome here as an addition to our crew,” my longtime friend James introduced me to his office and the rest of his employees. “This is our newest lawyer and soon to be partner, I hope and I would love it if everyone plays nice. He isn’t killing y’all yet so relax. Come with me.”
He led me to an empty office, “I hope your happy with this all.”
“I think it’s great and all but how do you get any work done? The staff is so small. I counted about seven people back there.”
“we have two other lawyers including me but it’s exactly why I wanted to get someone new. So your completely fine with working as a paralegal for now? I mean, you’re completely overqualified but if it’s what you want at the moment.”
“Yes its fine. No one else could offer as much as you have for just a graduate and it would mean some quick experience from you. I have no qualms with it but you did promise I would make partner someday. I’ll hold you on to that.”
“No problem, so I’ve got a case being run up at the moment and you couldn’t have come at a better time. I’d like for you to investigate a particular project being run by the company, Gigasonics. Apparently their causing a lot of ecological damage and a climate activists’ group wants to take it to court. They are the ones who hired us to represent them against the company. I would like you to follow up on their claims while I interview them. I’ll let you know what to look for and hopefully we get a case against them.”
And so my first assignment as a member of J.G & Co was underway. For all accounts I couldn’t just walk up to the company and ask for their records of areas where they might have caused environmental damage so I started with the newspapers of the dates James gave me and followed up at the areas themselves.
Each one of them were in a terrible state, abandoned buildings, farms and commercial ponds destroyed by lithium poisoning. The company didn’t do a very good job of disposing the product wastes and this is the outcome. I investigate all those who used to own these areas to get their approval and maybe get them to testify in court for them to get a shot at gaining something for all their work that got to waste.
Most of them were elderly people, very disappointed to get their only sources of income destroyed as well as their properties and had some very interesting things to say about the company in question however none of them were in any state to testify in a court of law so I had to make do with a written and signed affidavits.
“What did you think the company is going to do about it? I’m sure their bringing up their own case. They do have very good lawyers and I hear they’ve got a politician backing them too,” Faith said to me when I got back home after a long day of interviews.
“I’m sure they have got something up their sleeve but our case is airtight and I am sure James will do a good job. We’ve got this.”
“these guys usually got dirty ideas just running around.”
The next day I go to the office I found the clerical staff unusually quiet. I asked Nina, the receptionist, what was going on to warrant the unusual behavior.
“some men walked in a couple of minutes ago, Joshua said he recognized one of them as the lawyer on the Gigasonics team. They went into James’ office and haven’t come out yet. We think it’s something unusual.”
I walked to my office to arrange my files that I had prepared to present to James for his case but I couldn’t shake the feeling something was extremely wrong about the company’s behavior. With the case very much against them what sort of measures are they going to come up with.
After a few more minutes the lawyers left the office looking a bit nervous and I was able to present my findings to James.
“What were they looking for?” I asked
“They were looking for some way to sway my standing on the case.”
“They seem desperate, I suppose. I don’t see anyway how they might be able to change the outcome though. The case is pretty clear cut.”
“There’s something I need to let you know about though. Through my interviews with the activists themselves, few things popped up that may affect the case. The political standings of the president might cause things to go south. The problem is that the judge is highly political and if something goes wrong it’s in no way our fault. You understand this right.”
“why are you letting me know this?”
“I do need to remind you we’re a little short on cash right now.”
“tell me why you’re telling me this,” I said louder. I didn’t like the tone he was taking this. With everything we’re doing for the environment, the people who lost so much, our own futures. Why would anyone go this far? I don’t understand.
“I gave the information away.”
“For your own benefit. So you’re throwing the case away for a maybe future. You used to be an upstanding person.”
“The world isn’t so clear as you think it is Tim,” he raised his own voice to match mine.
“What about the promises you made to the activists, our team that works so hard to make the abusers pay. What do you think they would feel about this?”
He went silent. There was nothing to say anyway, the trust he built up for so many, as a leader it takes up so much. Maybe a I was rash in my decision but I said I couldn’t stand to be by his side in the courthouse. He did lose the case, but later on the company got more staff and more material. But he lost my respect.
Maybe the world may not be so nice and clean, but I wouldn’t love it if I didn’t try. So I discussed it with my girlfriend faith and decided to quit. There wouldn’t be any easy feat to get a job out there but I wouldn’t give up either. Maybe my naivety would lead to something worse but I defend my morals and principals, and that is my motto. No deception.
~~
“The more I think about it, a firm based on pure honesty sounds like exactly like you.”

title: The Day the Sun Disappeared
author: Sekani Msachi
content:
Two years ago, my father, Mr. Chigumula, was a teacher at Matemba Boys’ Private Secondary School in Mzuzu. He had three wives and six children. I was the youngest, and the only boy. We all lived together in one small three-bedroom house , and I say three bedrooms loosely, because the third was really a converted garage I shared with our chickens. That was my bedroom. Me and the chickens.
Life in the city was not cheap. My father was the only breadwinner, and no matter how hard he tried, his salary alone was never quite enough. My mother helped out with her fritters and doughnuts business, and during school holidays, my sisters and I would join her at the local market, and at church on Sundays, selling to whoever would buy. But it still wasn’t enough. The city had a way of eating money before you even had a chance to count it. So one day, my father made a decision. He said the village life was simpler, cheaper. He got himself a transfer to Kathondo Community Day Secondary School, a school deep in the hills of northern Mzimba.
Our house in Kathondo was old, but it was bigger. I finally had my own proper room , four walls, a door, no chickens. The school provided it for us. We were grateful. But gratitude is a quiet thing, and the people of Kathondo were loud in their suspicion. The fact that my father owned a car was enough for them to decide we thought ourselves above them. We didn’t even own the car outright , it was still giving him trouble at the mechanic’s most of the time , but to the village, a car was a car.
Two months in, my father was promoted to head teacher. I was not surprised. He had over twenty years of teaching experience behind him, and it showed in everything he did , the way he carried himself, the way he spoke, the way he handled a classroom. A dove among ravens, my grandmother used to say about people like him. His colleagues saw it too, and they hated him for it. Their resentment found its way to me. They sent me out of class for nothing. They gave me punishments that made no sense. One teacher once made me kneel in the sun for an entire afternoon because he claimed I was breathing too loudly during a lesson. When I told my father about these things, he would look at me with tired eyes and say, “Taleka my boy, if the teachers did that to you, then you deserved it. Stop whining like a starving dog and use that energy to become a good student.”
I learned quickly that there were two kinds of unfairness in this world. The kind that comes from strangers. And the kind that comes from people who are supposed to protect you.
About four weeks after my father’s promotion, two of my classmates died. Form 2 students, both of them. Nobody knew from what. The village grieved loudly, the way villages grieve , with wailing and prayers and nights that never seemed to end. But while the grief was still fresh, more children fell sick. Then more died. Week after week, without stopping. The witch doctors came. The herbalists came. Nothing helped. The sickness moved through the village like something with intention, choosing the young, leaving the old to watch.
It was then that the jealous teachers saw their chance. They began to whisper, and whispers in a small village carry farther than shouts. They said my father had cursed the village. That his arrival had brought death with it. That before we came, Kathondo had been at peace. People who had never spoken to us, who had never once looked us in the eye, were suddenly certain of our guilt. The bandwagon filled up fast.
One night, we were woken by voices outside. Many voices. Then stones. The first rock hit the window of the sitting room and glass scattered across the floor. My mother pulled us all into the corridor. My fathers’ wives clutched their children. More rocks came. Then I smelled it , smoke. They had set the car alight. Then the house.
We had no choice but to run outside.
The moment my father stepped out, they were on him. Four, maybe five men. My mother screamed and ran toward him, pulling at arms, begging. I ran too. I wanted to tear them away from him with my bare hands. But a man caught me from behind and held me in a grip I could not break. He was big. His arms were like iron. I struggled and screamed and kicked at the air, but I could not move. All I could do was watch.
I watched my father fall.
I watched him stop moving.
By dawn, half the village had come to look at us. Some had shame on their faces , but only some. Most of them stood around like it was a market day. They laughed. They pointed. They said things I will never repeat. Eventually the police came and took us to their station. The local chiefs helped track down my father’s parents in Nkhatabay, in a small village called Sambani, along the shores of Lake Malawi. Transport was arranged. Apologies were made. Promises of justice were made too. My father was buried in Kathondo soil, in a village that had killed him.
Sambani was not the fresh start we needed. It was simply a different kind of hard.
My grandparents already had a full house , children, grandchildren, aunts, uncles, the whole extended family pressing against the walls. We arrived and added ourselves to that number. I shared a bed with three cousins. I shared my clothes. I shared my plate. My sisters did the same. My mothers, still widows and still grieving, were quietly absorbed into uncles’ households in the way that sometimes happens to women when they have no man to stand beside them. Nobody asked them if that was what they wanted.
I stopped going to school. My grandfather needed help on the lake, and so I fished. We all fished. It was the only way to eat and earn something. I did not mind the water itself , there was something about the early mornings on the lake, the mist sitting low on the surface, the sound of oars, the weight of the net in your hands, that gave me a small, quiet peace. But at night, the peace left and the memories came. My father’s face. The way he looked on the ground. I would close my eyes and there he was, bruised and still. There were times, standing waist-deep in the lake, when I thought about just going under. Just letting the water take the weight of everything. But every time that thought crept in, I would hear my mother’s voice in my head , not her words, just the sound of her crying that night , and I would stay.
I told myself we had left Kathondo behind. That the story was done. But stories have a way of following you.
I started to catch pieces of conversation among the fishermen. Hushed things. Our names. Kathondo. A curse. The word witches. By the time the neighbors picked it up, it was no longer hushed. I stayed quiet. I told myself it was not worth the trouble. But then the children of Sambani started to die.
It was the same pattern , sudden fever, then gone. One child, then another. The village trembled with grief. And like before, like a terrible script being read a second time, the fingers turned toward us. People refused to buy our fish. Our income dried up. We could not afford soap, salt, paraffin for the lamp. The accusations grew louder as the deaths grew in number, until one Monday afternoon, a crowd gathered outside our house.
We locked the door and did not speak. We just listened to the voices outside calling for us to leave, to go, to take our curse with us.
Then, at midday, the sky changed.
It happened slowly at first , a dimming, like someone turning down a lamp. Then faster. The light drained from the afternoon until it was dark as evening, and the sun was completely gone from the sky. Just gone. I pressed my face to the window and watched the crowd outside freeze. Then scatter. They ran in every direction, stumbling over one another, disappearing into their houses, their voices now a different kind of loud , scared, not angry. Within minutes, the compound was empty.
About seven minutes later, the light came back. Slowly, the sun returned, and the day was itself again.
I knew what it was. I had read about it once in a physics book at my old school in Mzuzu , a solar eclipse, when the moon moves between the earth and the sun, blocking the light, throwing a shadow over the land. A completely natural thing. Timed by the universe, not by us. But the people outside did not know that, and when they came back , and they did come back, this time with sticks and torches and bricks , the eclipse had only made things worse. To them, we had called the darkness. We had swallowed the sun and spat it back out as a warning. We were witches, confirmed.
As I heard them return, the night in Kathondo played in my head like a film I could not stop. The same sounds. The same heat. Déjà vu so heavy it sat on my chest. Rocks hit the iron sheets above us, one after another, like hard rain. The windows went. The door shuddered with each blow, the hinges beginning to give. My family knelt and prayed. I sat and counted. Counted what, I don’t know. Time, maybe. Seconds. Breaths. How many we had left.
Then the sound of sirens.
Police vehicles came into the compound, blue lights cutting through the dust. Officers in uniform moved through the crowd with tear gas and rubber bullets. The crowd broke, though not completely , some stayed, still shouting, still throwing. A knock came at our door. My grandfather opened it. An officer stood there with a calm face and said they had come to help, and that we should come with them now.
We ran to the vehicles. As we pulled out, people chased us down the road, throwing whatever they could find. They followed us all the way to the police station. Even there, outside the gates, the crowd stood and sang songs of violence and threatened to tear down the walls. My heart was going so fast I thought it would give out. My mothers and sisters sat in a corner of the station, holding each other, shaking. I sat beside them. After hours , long, slow hours , the officers finally pushed the crowd back and made arrests. The compound went quiet.
I exhaled for the first time all day.
The next morning, officials from the Ministry of Health came to Sambani. Everyone was called to the chief’s courtyard. We went too, standing near the back, close to each other. The officials had results from laboratory tests conducted after the deaths in Kathondo the year before. They explained in careful, measured language that the children had died from cholera , a disease caused by a waterborne bacterium called Vibrio cholerae. It spread through contaminated water. It moved fast. It killed children first, because their immune systems were not yet strong enough to fight it.
The courtyard was very quiet when they finished. I watched faces in the crowd. Some people looked at the ground. Some looked at us. I did not look away.
The truth is a hard thing to give people when they have already decided what to believe. It took time. But eventually, slowly , the way the sun came back that afternoon , they accepted it.
My father was still gone. That part did not change.
But at least we knew, now, that the darkness had never really been ours to carry.
